patches.find: ESA (2022) provides parallax measurements for 1.8 billion stars. Parallax is inversely proportional to distance: Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly) shows 0.768 arcsec, Sirius (8.6 ly) shows 0.379 arcsec. A firmament wobble would produce identical angular displacement for all objects. Gaia conclusively demonstrates distance-dependent parallax.
patches.replace: Gaia Collaboration et al. (2023, A&A 674, A1) provides parallax measurements for 1.8 billion stars. Parallax is inversely proportional to distance: Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly) shows 0.768 arcsec, Sirius (8.6 ly) shows 0.379 arcsec — a 2× distance difference producing a 2× parallax difference. A rigid firmament wobble would produce one fixed angular displacement for all objects: at firmament height H = 9,086 km, a 20 m amplitude gives arctan(20 / 9,086,000) ≈ 0.454 arcsec regardless of target distance. Invoking a non-rigid or variable-amplitude wobble to mimic the 1/distance variation introduces an unfalsifiable free parameter. Gaia DR3 also cross-validates parallax using independent photometric distance methods (Cepheids, RR Lyrae, spectrophotometry) — both methods agree, ruling out any global systematic that would affect geometric parallax alone.
patches.rationale: ISS-267: Replace generic ESA link with specific Gaia Collaboration 2023 (A&A 674, A1) citation. ISS-268: Add note that non-rigid wobble defense introduces an unfalsifiable free parameter. ISS-269: Add the arctan(20/9,086,000) ≈ 0.454 arcsec calculation showing a rigid wobble produces one fixed shift, not a 1/d continuum. Also add cross-validation via photometric distances to preempt refraction escape.

patches.find: Equation of time ranges +16.5 to -14.1 min; 6.9 is cherry-picked RMS
patches.replace: Equation of time ranges +16.4 to −14.3 min; 6.9 is obliquity sub-component RMS only (full equation-of-time RMS = 8.8 min)
patches.rationale: ISS-166: Finding incorrectly states '+16.5 to -14.1 min' but the evidence says '+16.4 min (November) to −14.3 min (February)'. Fix to be consistent with the evidence. ISS-167: Clarify that '6.9 is cherry-picked RMS' means the obliquity sub-component RMS, not the full equation-of-time RMS (8.8 min). The old wording was ambiguous — a defender could read it as saying 6.9 IS the RMS.

patches.find: Gravitational torque produces exact 18.613-year period. Confirmed to 10 significant figures by lunar laser ranging. Epicyclic gears provide no physical driver.
patches.replace: Gravitational torque produces the exact 18.613-year nodal regression period. This cycle has been observed for over 2,000 years — it is encoded in the Antikythera mechanism (~100 BC) and appears in Babylonian eclipse records predating that. Modern lunar laser ranging (LLR) confirms lunar orbital parameters to millimeter precision. The dome's epicyclic gears provide no physical driver for the 18.613-year period and no mechanism to derive it from dome geometry.
patches.rationale: ISS-017: Remove unverified '10 significant figures' claim for LLR precision on this specific period. ISS-171: Replace 'known for 250 years' (implies ~1776) with accurate history — the nodal regression has been tracked since Babylonian times and is encoded in the Antikythera mechanism (~100 BC). ISS-172: Replace tangential LRR hardware link with better-sourced citation; improve description to clarify LLR confirms orbital parameters generally.

patches.find: Non-discriminating: period is an input (known for 250 years); dome's gears have no physical driver or amplitude prediction
patches.replace: Non-discriminating: period is an input (known for over 2,000 years, encoded in Antikythera mechanism ~100 BC); dome's gears have no physical driver or amplitude prediction
patches.rationale: ISS-171: 'Known for 250 years' implies ~1776. The 18.6-year nodal regression has been tracked since Babylonian antiquity and appears in the Antikythera mechanism (~100 BC). Correcting this prevents a dome defender from easily rebutting our historical claim.

patches.find: The ~950 AD date is taken from published paleomagnetic literature (Campuzano 2019), not derived from dome geometry. Linking it to a theological timeline without a physical mechanism is unfalsifiable.
patches.replace: The ~950 AD date is taken directly from published paleomagnetic literature (Campuzano et al. 2019) — post-hoc appropriation of a scientific result, not prediction. Linking it to a theological timeline (Revelation 20, 'Satan's little season') without deriving the date from dome parameters is unfalsifiable.
patches.rationale: ISS-178: Sharpen verdict framing. 'Taken from literature' is correct but 'post-hoc appropriation' is the precise epistemological term. Adding the specific theological reference (Revelation 20) makes the unfalsifiability critique more concrete and harder to dismiss.

patches.find: This is a theological interpretation, not a testable physical claim. No physical mechanism or measurable consequence is specified.
patches.replace: The dome model uses r = 0 as the mathematical North Pole in its cavity height formula H(r) and as the aetheric source in its toroidal flow model — those functional roles are addressed elsewhere in this review. WIN-031 specifically claims that six ancient texts (Genesis 1:2, Job 37:18, Isaiah 40:22, Genesis 1:6–8, Hildegard of Bingen's Scivias, and related sources) converge on a 'cosmic mountain' at the North Pole, constituting a confirmed 'prediction.' Two problems undermine this: (1) Textual exegesis of pre-existing documents is retrodiction, not prediction — these texts existed thousands of years before the dome model was written. (2) No physical mechanism specifies a measurable height, composition, or observable effect for the mountain that distinguishes dome geometry from any other cosmology whose axis passes through the North Pole.
patches.rationale: ISS-190: The 2-sentence evidence strawmanned the claim by ignoring the dome's functional use of r=0. Expand to acknowledge the dome's real assertion, identify the six texts (ISS-191), and then give the two-part rebuttal: retrodiction + no distinguishing prediction (ISS-192).

patches.find: Theological assertion, not testable physical claim.
patches.replace: Six-text convergence is retrodiction (all texts predate the model by centuries to millennia); no measurable physical consequence distinguishes this from any cosmology with a North Pole axis.
patches.rationale: ISS-192: The verdict text 'Theological assertion, not testable physical claim' is true but too brief. Updating to explicitly note the retrodiction problem and the lack of distinguishing prediction sharpens the critique.

patches.find: Theological assertion, not testable physical claim
patches.replace: Six-text convergence is retrodiction (texts predate the model); no measurable physical consequence distinguishes this from any cosmology with a North Pole axis
patches.rationale: ISS-192: Match summary table finding to the improved verdict framing.
